content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance
(916) 322-5662

Administration
322-5660

Executive/Legal
322-5901

Enforcement
322-6441

June 18, 1984


Joan Mihay
3485 E. Bellaire Way
Fresno, CA 93726

Re: Advice Letter No. A-84-132

Dear Ms. Mihay:

Thank you for your request for advice on the campaign
reporting provisions of the Political Reform Act.

QUESTION PRESENTED

How should a candidate or committee report contributions
from married contributors?

DISCUSSION

Government Code Section 82015L/ defines the term
"contribution" as "a payment ... except to the extent that full
and adequate consideration is received unless it is clear from
the surrounding circumstances that it is not made for political
purposes." Section 84211(f) specifies that:

* * *

(f) If the cumulative amount of contributions ...
received from a person is one hundred dollars ($100) or
more and a contribution or loan has been received from
that person during the period covered by the campaign
statement [a candidate's or committee's campaign
statement shall contain the following information
concerning the contributor]:

_________________

1/ Hereinafter all statutory references are to the
Government Code, unless otherwise indicated.

Joan Mihay
June 18, 1984
Page 2


(1) His or her full name;

(2) His or her street address;

(3) His or her occupation;

(4) The name of his or her employer or if
self-employed, name of business;

(5) The date and amount received for each
contribution received during the period covered by the
campaign statement and if the contribution is a loan,
the interest rate for the loan; and

(6) The cumulative amount of contributions.

The term "person," as used in Section 84211(f), includes "a
group of persons acting in concert."2/ Thus, a husband and
wife acting in concert qualify as a "person" under the Act.

If the cumulative amount of contributions received from
spouses amount to $100 or more, the contributions are required
to be reported as follows:

1. If a check is drawn on an account bearing the name of
only one spouse, only that spouse's name, street address and
occupation are required to be listed on the campaign reports.
However, the contribution must be aggregated with additional
contributions received from the husband, wife, or both.3/ In
cases where contributions are cumulated, the FPPC recommends
that the filer make a notation next to the reported contribution
indicating the names of contributors of other cumulated
contributions, in order to avoid confusion.

2. If a check is drawn on a joint account, the candidate or
committee is required to list the names and addresses of both

2/ Section 82047.

3/ This cumulation is required because, absent evidence
to the contrary, a contribution from a married individual is
presumed to be from joint rather than separate funds. This
presumption is refuted if the individual informs the candidate
or committee that the contribution is from legally separate
funds.

Joan Mihay
June 18, 1984
Page 3


spouses, and the occupation and employer of only one of the
spouses. However, the Commission is considering altering this
advice to require filers to report the occupations of both
spouses.

Thank you very much for your letter. If you have any
additional questions, please feel free to contact me at
(916) 322-5901.

Very truly yours,

Janis Shank McLean
Janis Shank McLean
Counsel
Legal Division

JSM:plh

May 15, 1984

Fair Political Practices Commission
Technical Assistance and Analysis Division
1100 K Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

It is my understanding that for FPPC reporting purposes, contri-
butions made by spouses are deemed as from a single source. I
cannot find this in the law itself and would appreciate your
citation re this.

I have reviewed many of the campaign reports filed in Fresno
city and county and note that only the husband is named although
I know the contribution came from both (e.g. a fundraiser where
both husband and wife attended.)

I have also noted other cases where even though the contribution,
loan or expenditure is designated as from or to a married couple,
only the husband is identified as to occupation.

It seems clear to me that a contribution from a couple should
identify both persons by name, address and occupation. Has the
Commission issued any ruling re this? If not I would urge you
to do so.

I would appreciate a written response as many politically active
women in this community are awaiting your reply.

Sincerely,

Joan Mihay
3485 E. Bellaire Way
Fresno, CA 93726

sections.question:
How should a candidate or committee report contributions 
from married contributors?
sections.conclusion: If a check is drawn on an account bearing the name of only one spouse, only that spouse's name, street address and occupation are required to be listed, but the contribution must be aggregated with other contributions from either spouse. If a check is drawn on a joint account, both spouses' names and addresses must be listed, along with the occupation and employer of only one spouse. The Commission noted it was considering requiring occupations of both spouses.
sections.analysis:

"contribution" as "a payment ••• except to the extent that full 
and adequate consideration is received unless it is clear from 
the surrounding circumstances that it is not made for political 
purposes. II 
Section 84211(f) specifies that:
sections.question_synthetic: The requester asks how contributions from married couples should be reported in campaign statements, citing confusion about whether spouses should be listed individually or as a single source.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advises that contributions from married couples are reported differently depending on the account type: single-name accounts require only that spouse's information (but must be aggregated with spouse's other contributions), while joint accounts require both spouses' names and addresses with only one spouse's occupation listed.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: old; LLM: Document includes both the incoming request (May 15, 1984) and FPPC response (June 18, 1984). Standard advice letter format with clearly marked QUESTION PRESENTED and DISCUSSION sections rather than separate CONCLUSION section, but conclusion content is identifiable in the DISCUSSION. Old format from 1984.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: How should a candidate or committee report contributions 
from married contributors?

CONCLUSION: If a check is drawn on an account bearing the name of only one spouse, only that spouse's name, street address and occupation are required to be listed, but the contribution must be aggregated with other contributions from either spouse. If a check is drawn on a joint account, both spouses' names and addresses must be listed, along with the occupation and employer of only one spouse. The Commission noted it was considering requiring occupations of both spouses.

embedding.summary: This advice letter responds to a requester's question about how contributions from married couples should be reported under California's Political Reform Act campaign reporting provisions. The FPPC provides guidance distinguishing between contributions from single-name versus joint accounts, with recommendations for aggregating spousal contributions.
